Thank God we are here. We've done all this effort up to this point to gather intelligence, to do what? To make smarter decisions. And so the first step of making smart decisions is to set goals based on history, not based on dreams of grandeur, but actual like, this is what is and this is what we are going to base our goals off of. I can tell you that doing it this way is why the goals that we have, we almost always achieve and overachieve because we do goal setting in this way. So that you can actually have, I mean, everyone's like smart goals, specific measurable blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, all those ones. But I mean like actually smart, intelligent goals that make sense based on data. Alright, so let's get jiggy. So I blew this up so that everyone could see it. So if you're looking over here, you see step one. Okay, so this is kind of your goal setting piece. So what I want you to do, all the yellows that you'll see here are inputs. Okay, the calculations already put in there for you. And then the green is going to be the output, it's going to be the valuable piece of intel. Alright, so let's say that your total overhead over the last three months was x. Okay, now there's two ways that you can include this for this goal. Okay, one is you can include advertising spend, which is what I would recommend. Or you can choose not to include advertising spend, because the goals that I set are based on recurring revenue, not total revenue, because the front end revenue is literally only there to liquidate acquisition. So if I can break even on the acquisition, I'm happy if I make one of the acquisition, I'm happy. The point is to build the recurring because that is what builds the stability of the business. And so my goals for our business are always based on the recurring revenue, because that is the structure that is the backbone of the business. Okay, so you can choose to either include ad spend in here or not include it. I would prefer to include it. It gives you an uglier picture, which makes you set better goals in my opinion. So let's say that your total overhead including ad spend over the last three months, if you average them, all right, because you looked at your bank statement and you looked at all the minuses was $26,000 a month. Okay, now let's say that your number of current EFTs is 130. And your average EFT price not what you charge people, but your actual average EFT price is 168. Okay, now, if we're looking at average EFT, so that's going to mean that this is just math that's already been done for you, that means that the average EFT net received was $21,840, which means that your net with recurring revenue only is minus 4,000. Boo, that's okay. You have other revenue streams that are coming in, but we want to build this, which means let's say that you want to make $12,000 a month from your service-based business. Okay, so that's your monthly net goal. So that means the difference between where you're at and the goal is $16,000. Okay, 160. So it's literally just adding the negative 4,000 to this to get you to 16 grand. Okay, which means that at this average EFT price, you would need to sign up 76 EFTs in order to make up the difference. But wait, we know that for every 75 EFTs, you're going to need to add a new employee, which adds payroll. So you're going to input what your average cost of a new employee is. So that might be, it might be 3,000 for you, it might be 3,100. And when you're doing this, you need to add in your payroll expenses. So in California, for example, it was 1.22 times whatever I was paying them. So I paid 22% on top of their payroll that covered all of the extra costs that went, that were associated with it. So know what that number is and know what a full-time employee would be 40 hours a week and put that cost here. Okay, so maybe it's $3,000. Okay, and the nice thing is you can see how these adjust. Okay. And so I actually put the percentage here. So the actual increases are calculated for you because we know that if we have 96 people, then that's 128% of this number, which means that your absolute increase in payroll would be this. This is taking into account that like, it's not going to happen at perfect 75% increments. Okay, that's why we have this number. Okay. So that means that we need to add an additional 23 EFTs to cover our increase in payroll to get our net profit. Okay, hopefully that makes sense, which means our new goal is 119 in order to get that $16,000 increase. Okay, because we have to accommodate for the next negative that we're at now, and we have to accommodate for the employees that we're going to have to hire with the increased amount of clients. And that's something that's called a variable cost because we have fixed costs. Everyone's like, man, they look at their overhead now and they're like, if I only had 300 more EFTs, I'd be making X. Not really because you'd need a whole bunch more staff in order to manage them. Right. And so that means it's actually going to have, you have to have overshoot your goal even more to get back to your net monthly goal. Okay, which means that if you were currently at 130 and you wanted to get to $12,000 a month in that, then you need to get your gym up to 249 EFTs at that average EFT price. Okay, mind you, if you're like, well, I charge more than 168. Awesome. Then it means that you're going to overshoot your goal. If you're like, well, I charge, you know, like, I also, you know, if I had, if I were at that point, I wouldn't be including the $4,000 a month in an aspect, great, then it means you're going to overshoot your goal. So it means that if 12,000 is your, is your net monthly goal now, and all the new signups you have are actually, you know, $30 more, then it means you're, you're going to be making an extra three grand there, you're going to make an extra four grand from your ad spend. And all of a sudden, now you're making 20 grand a month net instead of 12. Okay. But I like to set goals this way, because when you do them, when you set goals this way, you will crush them. And you will, you will, if you, if and when you hit them, you will have crushed your actual net goal. Okay. So the yellows are what you input. And then the rest of the equations that are in here will input your new total EFT goal. So now we take this total new EFT goal and the difference and we're going to reverse engineer our goals into actions. All right. So if we have 130 current clients and our new goals to 49, which we grabbed from over here, the difference is 119, which we already have. Right. Right. You can see, and you can see, is this realistic for me? Right. Of what like, and this will calculate everything that you need to do. Okay. So these inputs of conversion to EFT, the, the input of loss before start, which is dropouts, keep the names the same. Your closing percentage or show percentage or scheduling percentage, your average lead cost, all of those inputs that we collected over here. The reason we're doing that is that now we can make really, really, really good forecasting predictions on what we need to do to achieve our goals. It's like finally the point that is the point. Okay. So let's follow along here. So if we had 130 and our goal was 249, 119 is the difference. And let's say we wanted to do it in, I'm going to, I'm going to make this a nice realistic thing. So let's say eight months. Okay. So the means the goal per month is that we need to add 15 new recurring members per month. Okay. And let's say our turn is 5%. Okay. Cause we're doing, we're, we're, we're doing well here. And that's our three month average attrition. Okay. So that means that the month before our goal hitting this is going to be at 234. So the reason I do this is that I like to get the absolute amount of attrition that's going to happen. The very month, the month, the very before hitting the goal, which again, this is something that overshoots it because you're not going to have, if you have 5% attrition, you're not going to lose 12 people when you're at 130, right? That's the math there is only like seven, right? Because 5% of 130 is the, I don't know, six and a half or seven somewhere in there. Right. And so you're not going to have that, but it means you're going to again, overshoot your goal. So I like to take this attrition, but mind you, sometimes when it goes higher, your attrition goes up, which is why I like to take it from the month before we achieve our goal. Okay. Which means that the actual number of EFCs that are needed to sign up every month to include for attrition is going to be 27, not 15. We see how the goal gets bigger, right? And so let's say that we average over our three month average for referrals per month is one a week, which would be four per month, which means over the three month period, we got 12. Okay. So I don't want you to get confused here. This is just a three month average for referrals so that we can take those into account. If you're like, I don't know what my referrals is, then make it zero. Okay. So let's say that's four. And that's actually an input. So let's make sure that that's yellow. Okay. So this means that you're going to need 23 people from cold traffic per month. All right. So we know 65% are converted from the front end to the EFT, right? You sign up this many EFTs. That's how many you're going to need to sign up from cold traffic. You sign up 65%, which means you need to have 35 starts, net starts, right? We had 10% who drop out, which means we actually have to have 39 front end sales, right? And if you're closing 50%, then it means you need 75, 77 appointments to show. And if your closing percentage is 47, your show percentage is 47, then you need 164 appointments that are scheduled. And if you're scheduling 78% of your leads, then you need 211 leads. Okay. So is everyone digging this? Everyone making sense here? So that means that you're closing 39 sales off of 211 leads. All right. That's if these are the numbers for your facility. Now, let's say that your two month average lead cost is $18, meaning if you took the total spend over two months and you divided by the total amount of leads that you had, and you're then you're spending $18 per lead, then it means that you're going to spend $3,800 per month to achieve this goal, which means you need to spend $126 per day. And then if you want to get even more granular, which I recommend you do, look at over that last two months, how many of your ads were winners? A winner for me would be if I am within 25% of that average lead cost above that, or so basically, you take this 1.25 and you multiply that times this, which is the average. And so any any, any ads that produced 10 leads for under $23 would be a winner, right? If these were my stats. And so I'm going to count how many ads I made total, and then I'm going to see what percentage of them were winners. Let's say 33%. So one out of three was a winner, which means that I need to make 30 new ads a month. And if I'm breaking this into weekly, then it means I need to make roughly eight or nine new ads per week. And so that is how all of this comes to be. So you know that if you want to, given the numbers that already exist, if you want to get 119 new EFTs added to your true recurring revenue to get you to 249 and you want to do it in eight months, you're going to need to sign up 15 net per month, which means you need to sign up 27 actual EFTs, which means you're going to have to have 35 people start actually in weigh in, which means you need to have 39 sales because 10% are going to drop out, right? And then so I can go, I'll finish this. So from the 39 front end sales, that's off of 77 appointments you showed, which is off of 164 that's scheduled, which is off to 211 leads. Okay. So doing this, I liked reverse engineering it because this is how you can make forecasts and make good goals, but it's essentially you have 211 leads, 164 schedule, 77 show, 39 by 35 start, 23 convert into an EFT. And then I'm going to lose 12, which gets me to 15. And then that is my month. Okay. And so it would take me eight months. And I'm going to get there in that period of time. Makes sense. That's how you reverse engineer your goals. So this is how I forecast what we need. The nice beautiful thing here is that you also can know that if you're, if you're at 119 difference, that's almost 150, which means you're likely going to have two employees that are going to get added in. And at that point, and so one of the things with this goal setting that I covered here is that you could continue to do this process where you like it's cyclical because it's technically something that approaches. It's an asymptote. So it approaches infinity, it never gets perfect, because it just basically decreases the range. Because you could say, okay, well, 23 means it's another third of an employee. And which means my absolute increase of payroll is higher, but then it brings I have to have more EFTs. And so it's kind of a circle. But this is this is all back of napkin, because none of this stuff is static. So if you get too bogged down in the math, then this isn't going to be a good exercise for you. But I call it back of napkin math, because these are, these are all based on what is, and we do purposely conservative measurements on our plus side and very aggressive measurements on our downside, so that we can accommodate for the fact that as we grow, there are going to be breakages. And you'd be surprised and amazed at how accurate when you set your goals this way, and then you reverse engineer them into the actions that you need to take in order to achieve them, how well you will do. So that is why we use business intelligence and how we use it to reach our goals and reverse engineer our success.
